228: // ReflectorOptions configures a Reflector.
229: type ReflectorOptions struct {
230: 	// Name is the Reflector's name. If unset/unspecified, the name defaults to the closest source_file.go:line
231: 	// in the call stack that is outside this package.
232: 	Name string
233: 
234: 	// TypeDescription is the Reflector's type description. If unset/unspecified, the type description is defaulted
235: 	// using the following rules: if the expectedType passed to NewReflectorWithOptions was nil, the type description is
236: 	// "<unspecified>". If the expectedType is an instance of *unstructured.Unstructured and its apiVersion and kind fields
237: 	// are set, the type description is the string encoding of those. Otherwise, the type description is set to the
238: 	// go type of expectedType..
239: 	TypeDescription string
240: 
241: 	// ResyncPeriod is the Reflector's resync period. If unset/unspecified, the resync period defaults to 0
242: 	// (do not resync).
243: 	ResyncPeriod time.Duration
244: 
245: 	// MinWatchTimeout, if non-zero, defines the minimum timeout for watch requests send to kube-apiserver.
246: 	// However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid negative performance impact on controlplane.
247: 	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
248: 
249: 	// Clock allows tests to control time. If unset defaults to clock.RealClock{}
250: 	Clock clock.Clock
251: }
252: 
253: // NewReflectorWithOptions creates a new Reflector object which will keep the
254: // given store up to date with the server's contents for the given
255: // resource. Reflector promises to only put things in the store that
256: // have the type of expectedType, unless expectedType is nil. If
257: // resyncPeriod is non-zero, then the reflector will periodically
258: // consult its ShouldResync function to determine whether to invoke
259: // the Store's Resync operation; `ShouldResync==nil` means always
260: // "yes".  This enables you to use reflectors to periodically process
261: // everything as well as incrementally processing the things that
262: // change.
263: func NewReflectorWithOptions(lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, store ReflectorStore, options ReflectorOptions) *Reflector {
264: 	reflectorClock := options.Clock
265: 	if reflectorClock == nil {
266: 		reflectorClock = clock.RealClock{}
267: 	}
268: 	minWatchTimeout := defaultMinWatchTimeout
269: 	if options.MinWatchTimeout > defaultMinWatchTimeout {
270: 		minWatchTimeout = options.MinWatchTimeout
271: 	}
272: 	r := &Reflector{
273: 		name:            options.Name,
274: 		resyncPeriod:    options.ResyncPeriod,
275: 		minWatchTimeout: minWatchTimeout,
276: 		typeDescription: options.TypeDescription,
277: 		listerWatcher:   ToListerWatcherWithContext(lw),
278: 		store:           store,
279: 		// We used to make the call every 1sec (1 QPS), the goal here is to achieve ~98% traffic reduction when
280: 		// API server is not healthy. With these parameters, backoff will stop at [30,60) sec interval which is
281: 		// 0.22 QPS. If we don't backoff for 2min, assume API server is healthy and we reset the backoff.
282: 		backoffManager:    wait.NewExponentialBackoffManager(800*time.Millisecond, 30*time.Second, 2*time.Minute, 2.0, 1.0, reflectorClock),
283: 		clock:             reflectorClock,
284: 		watchErrorHandler: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext(DefaultWatchErrorHandler),
285: 		expectedType:      reflect.TypeOf(expectedType),
286: 	}
287: 
288: 	if r.name == "" {
289: 		r.name = naming.GetNameFromCallsite(internalPackages...)
290: 	}
291: 
292: 	if r.typeDescription == "" {
293: 		r.typeDescription = getTypeDescriptionFromObject(expectedType)
294: 	}
295: 
296: 	if r.expectedGVK == nil {
297: 		r.expectedGVK = getExpectedGVKFromObject(expectedType)
298: 	}
299: 
300: 	r.useWatchList = clientfeatures.FeatureGates().Enabled(clientfeatures.WatchListClient)
301: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kubernetes--8c9c67c0@8c9c67c000104450cfc5a5f48053a9a84b73cf93/-/blob/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go%3FL120-300"}

{"content":"120: // object ID, the notifications are delivered in order.  Because
121: // `ObjectMeta.UID` has no role in identifying objects, it is possible
122: // that when (1) object O1 with ID (e.g. namespace and name) X and
123: // `ObjectMeta.UID` U1 in the SharedInformer's local cache is deleted
124: // and later (2) another object O2 with ID X and ObjectMeta.UID U2 is
125: // created the informer's clients are not notified of (1) and (2) but
126: // rather are notified only of an update from O1 to O2. Clients that
127: // need to detect such cases might do so by comparing the `ObjectMeta.UID`
128: // field of the old and the new object in the code that handles update
129: // notifications (i.e. `OnUpdate` method of ResourceEventHandler).
130: //
131: // A client must process each notification promptly; a SharedInformer
132: // is not engineered to deal well with a large backlog of
133: // notifications to deliver.  Lengthy processing should be passed off
134: // to something else, for example through a
135: // `client-go/util/workqueue`.
136: //
137: // A delete notification exposes the last locally known non-absent
138: // state, except that its ResourceVersion is replaced with a
139: // ResourceVersion in which the object is actually absent.
140: type SharedInformer interface {
141: 	// AddEventHandler adds an event handler to the shared informer using
142: 	// the shared informer's resync period.  Events to a single handler are
143: 	// delivered sequentially, but there is no coordination between
144: 	// different handlers.
145: 	// It returns a registration handle for the handler that can be used to
146: 	// remove the handler again, or to tell if the handler is synced (has
147: 	// seen every item in the initial list).
148: 	//
149: 	// Contextual logging: AddEventHandlerWithOptions together with a logger in the options should be used instead of AddEventHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
150: 	AddEventHandler(handler ResourceEventHandler) (ResourceEventHandlerRegistration, error)
151: 	// AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod adds an event handler to the
152: 	// shared informer with the requested resync period; zero means
153: 	// this handler does not care about resyncs.  The resync operation
154: 	// consists of delivering to the handler an update notification
155: 	// for every object in the informer's local cache; it does not add
156: 	// any interactions with the authoritative storage.  Some
157: 	// informers do no resyncs at all, not even for handlers added
158: 	// with a non-zero resyncPeriod.  For an informer that does
159: 	// resyncs, and for each handler that requests resyncs, that
160: 	// informer develops a nominal resync period that is no shorter
161: 	// than the requested period but may be longer.  The actual time
162: 	// between any two resyncs may be longer than the nominal period
163: 	// because the implementation takes time to do work and there may
164: 	// be competing load and scheduling noise.
165: 	// It returns a registration handle for the handler that can be used to remove
166: 	// the handler again and an error if the handler cannot be added.
167: 	//
168: 	// Contextual logging: AddEventHandlerWithOptions together with a logger in the options should be used instead of AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod in code which supports contextual logging.
169: 	AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod(handler ResourceEventHandler, resyncPeriod time.Duration) (ResourceEventHandlerRegistration, error)
170: 	// AddEventHandlerWithOptions is a variant of AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod where
171: 	// all optional parameters are passed in a struct.
172: 	AddEventHandlerWithOptions(handler ResourceEventHandler, options HandlerOptions) (ResourceEventHandlerRegistration, error)
173: 	// RemoveEventHandler removes a formerly added event handler given by
174: 	// its registration handle.
175: 	// This function is guaranteed to be idempotent, and thread-safe.
176: 	RemoveEventHandler(handle ResourceEventHandlerRegistration) error
177: 	// GetStore returns the informer's local cache as a Store.
178: 	GetStore() Store
179: 	// GetController is deprecated, it does nothing useful
180: 	GetController() Controller
181: 	// Run starts and runs the shared informer, returning after it stops.
182: 	// The informer will be stopped when stopCh is closed.
183: 	//
184: 	// Contextual logging: RunWithContext should be used instead of Run in code which uses contextual logging.
185: 	Run(stopCh <-chan struct{})
186: 	// RunWithContext starts and runs the shared informer, returning after it stops.
187: 	// The informer will be stopped when the context is canceled.
188: 	RunWithContext(ctx context.Context)
189: 	// HasSynced returns true if the shared informer's store has been
190: 	// informed by at least one full LIST of the authoritative state
191: 	// of the informer's object collection.  This is unrelated to "resync".
192: 	//
193: 	// Note that this doesn't tell you if an individual handler is synced!!
194: 	// For that, please call HasSynced on the handle returned by
195: 	// AddEventHandler.
196: 	HasSynced() bool
197: 	// LastSyncResourceVersion is the resource version observed when last synced with the underlying
198: 	// store. The value returned is not synchronized with access to the underlying store and is not
199: 	// thread-safe.
200: 	LastSyncResourceVersion() string
201: 
202: 	// The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
203: 	// connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
204: 	// will backoff and retry.
205: 	//
206: 	// The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
207: 	// the error message at an appropriate level.
208: 	//
209: 	// There's only one handler, so if you call this multiple times, last one
210: 	// wins; calling after the informer has been started returns an error.
211: 	//
212: 	// The handler is intended for visibility, not to e.g. pause the consumers.
213: 	// The handler should return quickly - any expensive processing should be
214: 	// offloaded.
215: 	//
216: 	// Contextual logging: SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext should be used instead of SetWatchErrorHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
217: 	SetWatchErrorHandler(handler WatchErrorHandler) error
218: 
219: 	// SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext is a variant of SetWatchErrorHandler where
220: 	// the handler is passed an additional context parameter.
221: 	SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext(handler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext) error
222: 
223: 	// The TransformFunc is called for each object which is about to be stored.
224: 	//
225: 	// This function is intended for you to take the opportunity to
226: 	// remove, transform, or normalize fields. One use case is to strip unused
227: 	// metadata fields out of objects to save on RAM cost.
228: 	//
229: 	// Must be set before starting the informer.
230: 	//
231: 	// Please see the comment on TransformFunc for more details.
232: 	SetTransform(handler TransformFunc) error
233: 
234: 	// IsStopped reports whether the informer has already been stopped.
235: 	// Adding event handlers to already stopped informers is not possible.
236: 	// An informer already stopped will never be started again.
237: 	IsStopped() bool
238: }
239: 
240: // Opaque interface representing the registration of ResourceEventHandler for
241: // a SharedInformer. Must be supplied back to the same SharedInformer's
242: // `RemoveEventHandler` to unregister the handlers.
243: //
244: // Also used to tell if the handler is synced (has had all items in the initial
245: // list delivered).
246: type ResourceEventHandlerRegistration interface {
247: 	// HasSynced reports if both the parent has synced and all pre-sync
248: 	// events have been delivered.
249: 	HasSynced() bool
250: }
251: 
252: // Optional configuration options for [SharedInformer.AddEventHandlerWithOptions].
253: // May be left empty.
254: type HandlerOptions struct {
255: 	// Logger overrides the default klog.Background() logger.
256: 	Logger *klog.Logger
257: 
258: 	// ResyncPeriod requests a certain resync period from an informer. Zero
259: 	// means the handler does not care about resyncs. Not all informers do
260: 	// resyncs, even if requested. See
261: 	// [SharedInformer.AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod] for details.
262: 	//
263: 	// If nil, the default resync period of the shared informer is used.
264: 	ResyncPeriod *time.Duration
265: }
266: 
267: // SharedIndexInformer provides add and get Indexers ability based on SharedInformer.
268: type SharedIndexInformer interface {
269: 	SharedInformer
270: 	AddIndexers(indexers Indexers) error
271: 	GetIndexer() Indexer
272: }
273: 
274: // NewSharedInformer creates a new instance for the ListerWatcher. See NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions for full details.
275: func NewSharedInformer(lw ListerWatcher, exampleObject runtime.Object, defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod time.Duration) SharedInformer {
276: 	return NewSharedIndexInformer(lw, exampleObject, defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod, Indexers{})
277: }
278: 
279: // NewSharedIndexInformer creates a new instance for the ListerWatcher and specified Indexers. See
280: // NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions for full details.
281: func NewSharedIndexInformer(lw ListerWatcher, exampleObject runtime.Object, defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod time.Duration, indexers Indexers) SharedIndexInformer {
282: 	return NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions(
283: 		lw,
284: 		exampleObject,
285: 		SharedIndexInformerOptions{
286: 			ResyncPeriod: defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod,
287: 			Indexers:     indexers,
288: 		},
289: 	)
290: }
291: 
292: // NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions creates a new instance for the ListerWatcher.
293: // The created informer will not do resyncs if options.ResyncPeriod is zero.  Otherwise: for each
294: // handler that with a non-zero requested resync period, whether added
295: // before or after the informer starts, the nominal resync period is
296: // the requested resync period rounded up to a multiple of the
297: // informer's resync checking period.  Such an informer's resync
298: // checking period is established when the informer starts running,
299: // and is the maximum of (a) the minimum of the resync periods
300: // requested before the informer starts and the
301: // options.ResyncPeriod given here and (b) the constant
302: // `minimumResyncPeriod` defined in this file.
303: func NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions(lw ListerWatcher, exampleObject runtime.Object, options SharedIndexInformerOptions) SharedIndexInformer {
304: 	realClock := &clock.RealClock{}
305: 
306: 	processor := &sharedProcessor{clock: realClock}
307: 	processor.listenersRCond = sync.NewCond(processor.listenersLock.RLocker())
308: 
309: 	return &sharedIndexInformer{
310: 		indexer:                         NewIndexer(DeletionHandlingMetaNamespaceKeyFunc, options.Indexers),
311: 		processor:                       processor,
312: 		listerWatcher:                   lw,
313: 		objectType:                      exampleObject,
314: 		objectDescription:               options.ObjectDescription,
315: 		resyncCheckPeriod:               options.ResyncPeriod,
316: 		defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod: options.ResyncPeriod,
317: 		clock:                           realClock,
318: 		cacheMutationDetector:           NewCacheMutationDetector(fmt.Sprintf("%T", exampleObject)),
319: 		keyFunc:                         DeletionHandlingMetaNamespaceKeyFunc,
320: 	}
321: }
322: 
323: // SharedIndexInformerOptions configures a sharedIndexInformer.
324: type SharedIndexInformerOptions struct {
325: 	// ResyncPeriod is the default event handler resync period and resync check
326: 	// period. If unset/unspecified, these are defaulted to 0 (do not resync).
327: 	ResyncPeriod time.Duration
328: 
329: 	// Indexers is the sharedIndexInformer's indexers. If unset/unspecified, no indexers are configured.
330: 	Indexers Indexers
331: 
332: 	// ObjectDescription is the sharedIndexInformer's object description. This is passed through to the
333: 	// underlying Reflector's type description.
334: 	ObjectDescription string
335: }
336: 
337: // InformerSynced is a function that can be used to determine if an informer has synced.  This is useful for determining if caches have synced.
338: type InformerSynced func() bool
339: 
340: const (
341: 	// syncedPollPeriod controls how often you look at the status of your sync funcs
342: 	syncedPollPeriod = 100 * time.Millisecond
343: 
344: 	// initialBufferSize is the initial number of event notifications that can be buffered.
345: 	initialBufferSize = 1024
346: )

31: // DeltaFIFOOptions is the configuration parameters for DeltaFIFO. All are
32: // optional.
33: type DeltaFIFOOptions struct {
34: 
35: 	// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's
36: 	// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional
37: 	// handling around deleted objects and queue state).
38: 	// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.
39: 	KeyFunction KeyFunc
40: 
41: 	// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of
42: 	// this queue "knows about". It is used to decide which items are missing
43: 	// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.
44: 	// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().
45: 	KnownObjects KeyListerGetter
46: 
47: 	// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced indicates that the queue consumer
48: 	// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was
49: 	// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For
50: 	// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.
51: 	// When true, `Replaced` events will be sent for items passed to a Replace() call.
52: 	// When false, `Sync` events will be sent instead.
53: 	EmitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
54: 
55: 	// If set, will be called for objects before enqueueing them. Please
56: 	// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.
57: 	Transformer TransformFunc
58: 
59: 	// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().
60: 	Logger *klog.Logger
61: }
62: 
63: // DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the
64: // accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object
65: // but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that
66: // object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta
67: // except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the
68: // Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does
69: // not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new
70: // Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a
71: // DeletedFinalStateUnknown.
72: //
73: // The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that
74: // an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.
75: // If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in
76: // replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic
77: // resync events.
78: //
79: // DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is
80: // intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls
81: // the Pop() method.
82: //
83: // DeltaFIFO solves this use case:
84: //   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.
85: //   - When you process an object, you want to see everything
86: //     that's happened to it since you last processed it.
87: //   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.
88: //   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.
89: //
90: // DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return
91: // interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they
92: // will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns
93: // the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.
94: //
95: // A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities
96: // to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in
97: // question are called "known objects" and this set of objects
98: // modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods
99: // (each in a different way).
100: //
101: // A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple
102: // threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly
103: // different versions of the same object.
104: type DeltaFIFO struct {
105: 	// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.
106: 	lock sync.RWMutex
107: 	cond sync.Cond
108: 
109: 	// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.
110: 	// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.
111: 	items map[string]Deltas
112: 
113: 	// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().
114: 	// There are no duplicates in `queue`.
115: 	// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.
116: 	queue []string
117: 
118: 	// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated
119: 	// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.
120: 	populated bool
121: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kubernetes--8c9c67c0@8c9c67c000104450cfc5a5f48053a9a84b73cf93/-/blob/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/delta_fifo.go%3FL1-120"}

34: // New returns an http.RoundTripper that will provide the authentication
35: // or transport level security defined by the provided Config.
36: func New(config *Config) (http.RoundTripper, error) {
37: 	// Set transport level security
38: 	if config.Transport != nil && (config.HasCA() || config.HasCertAuth() || config.HasCertCallback() || config.TLS.Insecure) {
39: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("using a custom transport with TLS certificate options or the insecure flag is not allowed")
40: 	}
41: 
42: 	if !isValidHolders(config) {
43: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("misconfigured holder for dialer or cert callback")
44: 	}
45: 
46: 	var (
47: 		rt  http.RoundTripper
48: 		err error
49: 	)
50: 
51: 	if config.Transport != nil {
52: 		rt = config.Transport
53: 	} else {
54: 		rt, err = tlsCache.get(config)
55: 		if err != nil {
56: 			return nil, err
57: 		}
58: 	}
59: 
60: 	return HTTPWrappersForConfig(config, rt)
61: }
62: 
63: func isValidHolders(config *Config) bool {
64: 	if config.TLS.GetCertHolder != nil && config.TLS.GetCertHolder.GetCert == nil {
65: 		return false
66: 	}
67: 
68: 	if config.DialHolder != nil && config.DialHolder.Dial == nil {
69: 		return false
70: 	}
71: 
72: 	return true
73: }
74: 
75: // TLSConfigFor returns a tls.Config that will provide the transport level security defined
76: // by the provided Config. Will return nil if no transport level security is requested.
77: func TLSConfigFor(c *Config) (*tls.Config, error) {
78: 	if !(c.HasCA() || c.HasCertAuth() || c.HasCertCallback() || c.TLS.Insecure || len(c.TLS.ServerName) > 0 || len(c.TLS.NextProtos) > 0) {
79: 		return nil, nil
80: 	}

27: // Deprecated: Interface is deprecated, use TypedInterface instead.
28: type Interface TypedInterface[any]
29: 
30: type TypedInterface[T comparable] interface {
31: 	Add(item T)
32: 	Len() int
33: 	Get() (item T, shutdown bool)
34: 	Done(item T)
35: 	ShutDown()
36: 	ShutDownWithDrain()
37: 	ShuttingDown() bool
38: }
39: 
40: // Queue is the underlying storage for items. The functions below are always
41: // called from the same goroutine.
42: type Queue[T comparable] interface {
43: 	// Touch can be hooked when an existing item is added again. This may be
44: 	// useful if the implementation allows priority change for the given item.
45: 	Touch(item T)
46: 	// Push adds a new item.
47: 	Push(item T)
48: 	// Len tells the total number of items.
49: 	Len() int
50: 	// Pop retrieves an item.
51: 	Pop() (item T)
52: }
53: 
54: // DefaultQueue is a slice based FIFO queue.
55: func DefaultQueue[T comparable]() Queue[T] {
56: 	return new(queue[T])
57: }
58: 
59: // queue is a slice which implements Queue.
60: type queue[T comparable] []T
61: 
62: func (q *queue[T]) Touch(item T) {}
63: 
64: func (q *queue[T]) Push(item T) {
65: 	*q = append(*q, item)
66: }
67: 
68: func (q *queue[T]) Len() int {
69: 	return len(*q)
70: }
71: 
72: func (q *queue[T]) Pop() (item T) {
73: 	item = (*q)[0]
74: 
75: 	// The underlying array still exists and reference this object, so the object will not be garbage collected.
76: 	(*q)[0] = *new(T)
77: 	*q = (*q)[1:]
78: 
79: 	return item
80: }
81: 
82: // QueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize an Interface.
83: // Deprecated: use TypedQueueConfig instead.
84: type QueueConfig = TypedQueueConfig[any]
85: 
86: type TypedQueueConfig[T comparable] struct {
87: 	// Name for the queue. If unnamed, the metrics will not be registered.
88: 	Name string
89: 
90: 	// MetricsProvider optionally allows specifying a metrics provider to use for the queue
91: 	// instead of the global provider.
92: 	MetricsProvider MetricsProvider
93: 
94: 	// Clock ability to inject real or fake clock for testing purposes.
95: 	Clock clock.WithTicker
96: 
97: 	// Queue provides the underlying queue to use. It is optional and defaults to slice based FIFO queue.
98: 	Queue Queue[T]
99: }

21: // RateLimitingInterface is an interface that rate limits items being added to the queue.
22: //
23: // Deprecated: Use TypedRateLimitingInterface instead.
24: type RateLimitingInterface TypedRateLimitingInterface[any]
25: 
26: // TypedRateLimitingInterface is an interface that rate limits items being added to the queue.
27: type TypedRateLimitingInterface[T comparable] interface {
28: 	TypedDelayingInterface[T]
29: 
30: 	// AddRateLimited adds an item to the workqueue after the rate limiter says it's ok
31: 	AddRateLimited(item T)
32: 
33: 	// Forget indicates that an item is finished being retried.  Doesn't matter whether it's for perm failing
34: 	// or for success, we'll stop the rate limiter from tracking it.  This only clears the `rateLimiter`, you
35: 	// still have to call `Done` on the queue.
36: 	Forget(item T)
37: 
38: 	// NumRequeues returns back how many times the item was requeued
39: 	NumRequeues(item T) int
40: }
41: 
42: // RateLimitingQueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize a RateLimitingInterface.
43: //
44: // Deprecated: Use TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig instead.
45: type RateLimitingQueueConfig = TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[any]
46: 
47: // TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize a TypedRateLimitingInterface.
48: type TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[T comparable] struct {
49: 	// Name for the queue. If unnamed, the metrics will not be registered.
50: 	Name string
51: 
52: 	// MetricsProvider optionally allows specifying a metrics provider to use for the queue
53: 	// instead of the global provider.
54: 	MetricsProvider MetricsProvider
55: 
56: 	// Clock optionally allows injecting a real or fake clock for testing purposes.
57: 	Clock clock.WithTicker
58: 
59: 	// DelayingQueue optionally allows injecting custom delaying queue DelayingInterface instead of the default one.
60: 	DelayingQueue TypedDelayingInterface[T]
61: }
62: 
63: // NewRateLimitingQueue constructs a new workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
64: // Remember to call Forget!  If you don't, you may end up tracking failures forever.
65: // NewRateLimitingQueue does not emit metrics. For use with a MetricsProvider, please use
66: // NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead and specify a name.
67: //
68: // Deprecated: Use NewTypedRateLimitingQueue instead.
69: func NewRateLimitingQueue(rateLimiter RateLimiter) RateLimitingInterface {
70: 	return NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, RateLimitingQueueConfig{})
71: }
72: 
73: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueue constructs a new workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
74: // Remember to call Forget!  If you don't, you may end up tracking failures forever.
75: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueue does not emit metrics. For use with a MetricsProvider, please use
76: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead and specify a name.
77: func NewTypedRateLimitingQueue[T comparable](rateLimiter TypedRateLimiter[T]) TypedRateLimitingInterface[T] {
78: 	return NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[T]{})
79: }

17: // Package leaderelection implements leader election of a set of endpoints.
18: // It uses an annotation in the endpoints object to store the record of the
19: // election state. This implementation does not guarantee that only one
20: // client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing).
21: //
22: // A client only acts on timestamps captured locally to infer the state of the
23: // leader election. The client does not consider timestamps in the leader
24: // election record to be accurate because these timestamps may not have been
25: // produced by a local clock. The implemention does not depend on their
26: // accuracy and only uses their change to indicate that another client has
27: // renewed the leader lease. Thus the implementation is tolerant to arbitrary
28: // clock skew, but is not tolerant to arbitrary clock skew rate.
29: //
30: // However the level of tolerance to skew rate can be configured by setting
31: // RenewDeadline and LeaseDuration appropriately. The tolerance expressed as a
32: // maximum tolerated ratio of time passed on the fastest node to time passed on
33: // the slowest node can be approximately achieved with a configuration that sets
34: // the same ratio of LeaseDuration to RenewDeadline. For example if a user wanted
35: // to tolerate some nodes progressing forward in time twice as fast as other nodes,
36: // the user could set LeaseDuration to 60 seconds and RenewDeadline to 30 seconds.
37: //
38: // While not required, some method of clock synchronization between nodes in the
39: // cluster is highly recommended. It's important to keep in mind when configuring
40: // this client that the tolerance to skew rate varies inversely to master
41: // availability.
42: //
43: // Larger clusters often have a more lenient SLA for API latency. This should be
44: // taken into account when configuring the client. The rate of leader transitions
45: // should be monitored and RetryPeriod and LeaseDuration should be increased
46: // until the rate is stable and acceptably low. It's important to keep in mind
47: // when configuring this client that the tolerance to API latency varies inversely
48: // to master availability.
49: //
50: // DISCLAIMER: this is an alpha API. This library will likely change significantly
51: // or even be removed entirely in subsequent releases. Depend on this API at
52: // your own risk.
53: package leaderelection
54: 
55: import (
56: 	"bytes"
57: 	"context"
58: 	"fmt"
59: 	"sync"
60: 	"time"
61: 
62: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
63: 	metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
64: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
65: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait"
66: 	rl "k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock"
67: 	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
68: 	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
69: )
70: 
71: const (
72: 	JitterFactor = 1.2
73: )
74: 
75: // NewLeaderElector creates a LeaderElector from a LeaderElectionConfig
76: func NewLeaderElector(lec LeaderElectionConfig) (*LeaderElector, error) {
77: 	if lec.LeaseDuration <= lec.RenewDeadline {
78: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("leaseDuration must be greater than renewDeadline")
79: 	}
80: 	if lec.RenewDeadline <= time.Duration(JitterFactor*float64(lec.RetryPeriod)) {
81: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than retryPeriod*JitterFactor")
82: 	}
83: 	if lec.LeaseDuration < 1 {
84: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("leaseDuration must be greater than zero")
85: 	}
86: 	if lec.RenewDeadline < 1 {
87: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than zero")
88: 	}
89: 	if lec.RetryPeriod < 1 {
90: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("retryPeriod must be greater than zero")
91: 	}
92: 	if lec.Callbacks.OnStartedLeading == nil {
93: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStartedLeading callback must not be nil")
94: 	}
95: 	if lec.Callbacks.OnStoppedLeading == nil {
96: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStoppedLeading callback must not be nil")
97: 	}
98: 
99: 	if lec.Lock == nil {
100: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Lock must not be nil.")

33: // This file implements a low-level controller that is used in
34: // sharedIndexInformer, which is an implementation of
35: // SharedIndexInformer.  Such informers, in turn, are key components
36: // in the high level controllers that form the backbone of the
37: // Kubernetes control plane.  Look at those for examples, or the
38: // example in
39: // https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/tree/master/examples/workqueue
40: // .
41: 
42: // Config contains all the settings for one of these low-level controllers.
43: type Config struct {
44: 	// The queue for your objects - has to be a DeltaFIFO due to
45: 	// assumptions in the implementation. Your Process() function
46: 	// should accept the output of this Queue's Pop() method.
47: 	Queue
48: 
49: 	// Something that can list and watch your objects.
50: 	ListerWatcher
51: 
52: 	// Process can process a popped Deltas.
53: 	Process ProcessFunc
54: 
55: 	// ProcessBatch can process a batch of popped Deltas, which should return `TransactionError` if not all items
56: 	// in the batch were successfully processed.
57: 	//
58: 	// For batch processing to be used:
59: 	// * ProcessBatch must be non-nil
60: 	// * Queue must implement QueueWithBatch
61: 	// * The client InOrderInformersBatchProcess feature gate must be enabled
62: 	//
63: 	// If any of those are false, Process is used and no batch processing is done.
64: 	ProcessBatch ProcessBatchFunc
65: 
66: 	// ObjectType is an example object of the type this controller is
67: 	// expected to handle.
68: 	ObjectType runtime.Object
69: 
70: 	// ObjectDescription is the description to use when logging type-specific information about this controller.
71: 	ObjectDescription string
72: 
73: 	// FullResyncPeriod is the period at which ShouldResync is considered.
74: 	FullResyncPeriod time.Duration
75: 
76: 	// MinWatchTimeout, if set, will define the minimum timeout for watch requests send
77: 	// to kube-apiserver. However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid
78: 	// negative performance impact on controlplane.
79: 	// Optional - if unset a default value of 5m will be used.
80: 	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
81: 
82: 	// ShouldResync is periodically used by the reflector to determine
83: 	// whether to Resync the Queue. If ShouldResync is `nil` or
84: 	// returns true, it means the reflector should proceed with the
85: 	// resync.
86: 	ShouldResync ShouldResyncFunc
87: 
88: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
89: 	//
90: 	// Contextual logging: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext should be used instead of WatchErrorHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
91: 	WatchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandler
92: 
93: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error
94: 	// and WatchErrorHandler is not set.
95: 	WatchErrorHandlerWithContext WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
96: 
97: 	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and relist watch lists.
98: 	WatchListPageSize int64
99: }
100: 
101: // ShouldResyncFunc is a type of function that indicates if a reflector should perform a
102: // resync or not. It can be used by a shared informer to support multiple event handlers with custom
103: // resync periods.
104: type ShouldResyncFunc func() bool
105: 
106: // ProcessFunc processes a single object.
107: type ProcessFunc func(obj interface{}, isInInitialList bool) error
108: 
109: // ProcessBatchFunc processes multiple objects in batch.
110: // The deltas must not contain multiple entries for the same object.
111: type ProcessBatchFunc func(deltas []Delta, isInInitialList bool) error
112: 
113: // `*controller` implements Controller
114: type controller struct {
115: 	config         Config
116: 	reflector      *Reflector
117: 	reflectorMutex sync.RWMutex
118: 	clock          clock.Clock
119: }

54: // SharedInformerOption defines the functional option type for SharedInformerFactory.
55: type SharedInformerOption func(*sharedInformerFactory) *sharedInformerFactory
56: 
57: type sharedInformerFactory struct {
58: 	client           kubernetes.Interface
59: 	namespace        string
60: 	tweakListOptions internalinterfaces.TweakListOptionsFunc
61: 	lock             sync.Mutex
62: 	defaultResync    time.Duration
63: 	customResync     map[reflect.Type]time.Duration
64: 	transform        cache.TransformFunc
65: 
66: 	informers map[reflect.Type]cache.SharedIndexInformer
67: 	// startedInformers is used for tracking which informers have been started.
68: 	// This allows Start() to be called multiple times safely.
69: 	startedInformers map[reflect.Type]bool
70: 	// wg tracks how many goroutines were started.
71: 	wg sync.WaitGroup
72: 	// shuttingDown is true when Shutdown has been called. It may still be running
73: 	// because it needs to wait for goroutines.
74: 	shuttingDown bool
75: }
76: 
77: // WithCustomResyncConfig sets a custom resync period for the specified informer types.
78: func WithCustomResyncConfig(resyncConfig map[v1.Object]time.Duration) SharedInformerOption {
79: 	return func(factory *sharedInformerFactory) *sharedInformerFactory {
80: 		for k, v := range resyncConfig {
81: 			factory.customResync[reflect.TypeOf(k)] = v
82: 		}
83: 		return factory
84: 	}
85: }
86: 
87: // WithTweakListOptions sets a custom filter on all listers of the configured SharedInformerFactory.
88: func WithTweakListOptions(tweakListOptions internalinterfaces.TweakListOptionsFunc) SharedInformerOption {
89: 	return func(factory *sharedInformerFactory) *sharedInformerFactory {
90: 		factory.tweakListOptions = tweakListOptions
91: 		return factory
92: 	}
93: }
94: 
95: // WithNamespace limits the SharedInformerFactory to the specified namespace.
96: func WithNamespace(namespace string) SharedInformerOption {
97: 	return func(factory *sharedInformerFactory) *sharedInformerFactory {
98: 		factory.namespace = namespace
99: 		return factory
100: 	}
101: }
102: 
103: // WithTransform sets a transform on all informers.
104: func WithTransform(transform cache.TransformFunc) SharedInformerOption {
105: 	return func(factory *sharedInformerFactory) *sharedInformerFactory {
106: 		factory.transform = transform
107: 		return factory
108: 	}
109: }
110: 
111: // NewSharedInformerFactory constructs a new instance of sharedInformerFactory for all namespaces.
112: func NewSharedInformerFactory(client kubernetes.Interface, defaultResync time.Duration) SharedInformerFactory {
113: 	return NewSharedInformerFactoryWithOptions(client, defaultResync)
114: }

17: package cache
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"errors"
21: 	"fmt"
22: 	"strings"
23: 
24: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/meta"
25: 	metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"

28: // Store is a generic object storage and processing interface.  A
29: // Store holds a map from string keys to accumulators, and has
30: // operations to add, update, and delete a given object to/from the
31: // accumulator currently associated with a given key.  A Store also
32: // knows how to extract the key from a given object, so many operations
33: // are given only the object.
34: //
35: // In the simplest Store implementations each accumulator is simply
36: // the last given object, or empty after Delete, and thus the Store's
37: // behavior is simple storage.
38: //
39: // Reflector knows how to watch a server and update a Store.  This
40: // package provides a variety of implementations of Store.
41: type Store interface {
42: 
43: 	// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key
44: 	Add(obj interface{}) error
45: 
46: 	// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key
47: 	Update(obj interface{}) error
48: 
49: 	// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key
50: 	Delete(obj interface{}) error
51: 
52: 	// List returns a list of all the currently non-empty accumulators
53: 	List() []interface{}
54: 
55: 	// ListKeys returns a list of all the keys currently associated with non-empty accumulators
56: 	ListKeys() []string
57: 
58: 	// Get returns the accumulator associated with the given object's key
59: 	Get(obj interface{}) (item interface{}, exists bool, err error)
60: 
61: 	// GetByKey returns the accumulator associated with the given key
62: 	GetByKey(key string) (item interface{}, exists bool, err error)
63: 
64: 	// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the
65: 	// given list. Store takes ownership of the list, you should not reference
66: 	// it after calling this function.
67: 	Replace([]interface{}, string) error
68: 
69: 	// Resync is meaningless in the terms appearing here but has
70: 	// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial
71: 	// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).
72: 	Resync() error
73: }
74: 
75: // TransactionType defines the type of a transaction operation. It is used to indicate whether
76: // an object is being added, updated, or deleted.
77: type TransactionType string
78: 
79: const (
80: 	TransactionTypeAdd    TransactionType = "Add"
81: 	TransactionTypeUpdate TransactionType = "Update"
82: 	TransactionTypeDelete TransactionType = "Delete"
83: )
84: 
85: // Transaction represents a single operation or event in a process. It holds a generic Object
86: // associated with the transaction and a Type indicating the kind of transaction being performed.
87: type Transaction struct {
88: 	Object interface{}
89: 	Type   TransactionType
90: }
91: 
92: type TransactionStore interface {
93: 	// Transaction allows multiple operations to occur within a single lock acquisition to
94: 	// ensure progress can be made when there is contention.
95: 	Transaction(txns ...Transaction) *TransactionError
96: }
97: 
98: var _ error = &TransactionError{}
99: 
100: type TransactionError struct {

25: 
26: // Indexer extends Store with multiple indices and restricts each
27: // accumulator to simply hold the current object (and be empty after
28: // Delete).
29: //
30: // There are three kinds of strings here:
31: //  1. a storage key, as defined in the Store interface,
32: //  2. a name of an index, and
33: //  3. an "indexed value", which is produced by an IndexFunc and
34: //     can be a field value or any other string computed from the object.
35: type Indexer interface {
36: 	Store
37: 	// Index returns the stored objects whose set of indexed values
38: 	// intersects the set of indexed values of the given object, for
39: 	// the named index
40: 	Index(indexName string, obj interface{}) ([]interface{}, error)
41: 	// IndexKeys returns the storage keys of the stored objects whose
42: 	// set of indexed values for the named index includes the given
43: 	// indexed value
44: 	IndexKeys(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]string, error)
45: 	// ListIndexFuncValues returns all the indexed values of the given index
46: 	ListIndexFuncValues(indexName string) []string
47: 	// ByIndex returns the stored objects whose set of indexed values
48: 	// for the named index includes the given indexed value
49: 	ByIndex(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]interface{}, error)
50: 	// GetIndexers return the indexers
51: 	GetIndexers() Indexers
52: 
53: 	// AddIndexers adds more indexers to this store. This supports adding indexes after the store already has items.
54: 	AddIndexers(newIndexers Indexers) error
55: }
56: 
57: // IndexFunc knows how to compute the set of indexed values for an object.
58: type IndexFunc func(obj interface{}) ([]string, error)
59: 
60: // IndexFuncToKeyFuncAdapter adapts an indexFunc to a keyFunc.  This is only useful if your index function returns
61: // unique values for every object.  This conversion can create errors when more than one key is found.  You
62: // should prefer to make proper key and index functions.
63: func IndexFuncToKeyFuncAdapter(indexFunc IndexFunc) KeyFunc {
64: 	return func(obj interface{}) (string, error) {
65: 		indexKeys, err := indexFunc(obj)
66: 		if err != nil {
67: 			return "", err
68: 		}
69: 		if len(indexKeys) > 1 {
70: 			return "", fmt.Errorf("too many keys: %v", indexKeys)
71: 		}
72: 		if len(indexKeys) == 0 {
73: 			return "", fmt.Errorf("unexpected empty indexKeys")
74: 		}
75: 		return indexKeys[0], nil
76: 	}
77: }
78: 
79: const (
80: 	// NamespaceIndex is the lookup name for the most common index function, which is to index by the namespace field.
81: 	NamespaceIndex string = "namespace"
82: )
83: 
84: // MetaNamespaceIndexFunc is a default index function that indexes based on an object's namespace
85: func MetaNamespaceIndexFunc(obj interface{}) ([]string, error) {
86: 	meta, err := meta.Accessor(obj)
87: 	if err != nil {
88: 		return []string{""}, fmt.Errorf("object has no meta: %v", err)
89: 	}
90: 	return []string{meta.GetNamespace()}, nil
91: }
92: 
93: // Index maps the indexed value to a set of keys in the store that match on that value
94: type index map[string]sets.Set[string]
95: 
96: // Indexers maps a name to an IndexFunc
97: type Indexers map[string]IndexFunc
98: 
99: // Indices maps a name to an Index
100: type Indices map[string]index

27: // Deprecated: RateLimiter is deprecated, use TypedRateLimiter instead.
28: type RateLimiter TypedRateLimiter[any]
29: 
30: type TypedRateLimiter[T comparable] interface {
31: 	// When gets an item and gets to decide how long that item should wait
32: 	When(item T) time.Duration
33: 	// Forget indicates that an item is finished being retried.  Doesn't matter whether it's for failing
34: 	// or for success, we'll stop tracking it
35: 	Forget(item T)
36: 	// NumRequeues returns back how many failures the item has had
37: 	NumRequeues(item T) int
38: }
39: 
40: // DefaultControllerRateLimiter is a no-arg constructor for a default rate limiter for a workqueue.  It has
41: // both overall and per-item rate limiting.  The overall is a token bucket and the per-item is exponential
42: //
43: // Deprecated: Use DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter instead.
44: func DefaultControllerRateLimiter() RateLimiter {
45: 	return DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter[any]()
46: }
47: 
48: // DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter is a no-arg constructor for a default rate limiter for a workqueue.  It has
49: // both overall and per-item rate limiting.  The overall is a token bucket and the per-item is exponential
50: func DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter[T comparable]() TypedRateLimiter[T] {
51: 	return NewTypedMaxOfRateLimiter(
52: 		NewTypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter[T](5*time.Millisecond, 1000*time.Second),
53: 		// 10 qps, 100 bucket size.  This is only for retry speed and its only the overall factor (not per item)
54: 		&TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]{Limiter: rate.NewLimiter(rate.Limit(10), 100)},
55: 	)
56: }
57: 
58: // Deprecated: BucketRateLimiter is deprecated, use TypedBucketRateLimiter instead.

62: type TypedBucketRateLimiter[T comparable] struct {
63: 	*rate.Limiter
64: }

68: func (r *TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]) When(item T) time.Duration {
69: 	return r.Limiter.Reserve().Delay()
70: }
71: 
72: func (r *TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]) NumRequeues(item T) int {

74: }
75:

77: }

97: }

{"content":"350: // either a successful or failed sync.
351: //
352: // Contextual logging: WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext should be used instead of WaitForNamedCacheSync in code which supports contextual logging.
353: func WaitForNamedCacheSync(controllerName string, stopCh <-chan struct{}, cacheSyncs ...InformerSynced) bool {
354: 	klog.Background().Info("Waiting for caches to sync", "controller", controllerName)
355: 
356: 	if !WaitForCacheSync(stopCh, cacheSyncs...) {
357: 		utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext(context.Background(), nil, "Unable to sync caches", "controller", controllerName)
358: 		return false
359: 	}
360: 
361: 	klog.Background().Info("Caches are synced", "controller", controllerName)
362: 	return true
363: }
364: 
365: // WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext is a wrapper around WaitForCacheSyncWithContext that generates log messages
366: // indicating that the caller is waiting for syncs, followed by either a successful or failed sync.
367: //
368: // Contextual logging can be used to identify the caller in those log messages. The log level is zero,
369: // the same as in [WaitForNamedCacheSync]. If this is too verbose, then store a logger with an increased
370: // threshold in the context:
371: //
372: //	WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext(klog.NewContext(ctx, logger.V(5)), ...)
373: func WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext(ctx context.Context, cacheSyncs ...InformerSynced) bool {
374: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
375: 	logger.Info("Waiting for caches to sync")
376: 
377: 	if !WaitForCacheSync(ctx.Done(), cacheSyncs...) {
378: 		utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext(ctx, nil, "Unable to sync caches")
379: 		return false
380: 	}
381: 
382: 	logger.Info("Caches are synced")
383: 	return true
384: }
385: 
386: // WaitForCacheSync waits for caches to populate.  It returns true if it was successful, false
387: // if the controller should shutdown
388: // callers should prefer WaitForNamedCacheSync()
389: func WaitForCacheSync(stopCh <-chan struct{}, cacheSyncs ...InformerSynced) bool {
390: 	err := wait.PollImmediateUntil(syncedPollPeriod,
391: 		func() (bool, error) {
392: 			for _, syncFunc := range cacheSyncs {
393: 				if !syncFunc() {
394: 					return false, nil
395: 				}
396: 			}
397: 			return true, nil
398: 		},
399: 		stopCh)
400: 	if err != nil {
401: 		return false
402: 	}
403: 
404: 	return true
405: }
406: 
407: // `*sharedIndexInformer` implements SharedIndexInformer and has three
408: // main components.  One is an indexed local cache, `indexer Indexer`.
409: // The second main component is a Controller that pulls
410: // objects/notifications using the ListerWatcher and pushes them into
411: // a DeltaFIFO --- whose knownObjects is the informer's local cache
412: // --- while concurrently Popping Deltas values from that fifo and
413: // processing them with `sharedIndexInformer::HandleDeltas`.  Each
414: // invocation of HandleDeltas, which is done with the fifo's lock
415: // held, processes each Delta in turn.  For each Delta this both
416: // updates the local cache and stuffs the relevant notification into
417: // the sharedProcessor.  The third main component is that
418: // sharedProcessor, which is responsible for relaying those
419: // notifications to each of the informer's clients.
420: type sharedIndexInformer struct {
421: 	indexer    Indexer
422: 	controller Controller
423: 
424: 	processor             *sharedProcessor
425: 	cacheMutationDetector MutationDetector
426: 
427: 	listerWatcher ListerWatcher
428: 
429: 	// objectType is an example object of the type this informer is expected to handle. If set, an event
430: 	// with an object with a mismatching type is dropped instead of being delivered to listeners.
431: 	objectType runtime.Object
432: 
433: 	// objectDescription is the description of this informer's objects. This typically defaults to
434: 	objectDescription string
435: 
436: 	// resyncCheckPeriod is how often we want the reflector's resync timer to fire so it can call
437: 	// shouldResync to check if any of our listeners need a resync.
438: 	resyncCheckPeriod time.Duration
439: 	// defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod is the default resync period for any handlers added via
440: 	// AddEventHandler (i.e. they don't specify one and just want to use the shared informer's default
441: 	// value).
442: 	defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod time.Duration
443: 	// clock allows for testability
444: 	clock clock.Clock
445: 
446: 	started, stopped bool
447: 	startedLock      sync.Mutex
448: 
449: 	// blockDeltas gives a way to stop all event distribution so that a late event handler
450: 	// can safely join the shared informer.
451: 	blockDeltas sync.Mutex
452: 
453: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
454: 	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
455: 
456: 	transform TransformFunc
457: 
458: 	// keyFunc is called when processing deltas by the underlying process function.
459: 	keyFunc KeyFunc
460: }
461: 
462: // dummyController hides the fact that a SharedInformer is different from a dedicated one
463: // where a caller can `Run`.  The run method is disconnected in this case, because higher
464: // level logic will decide when to start the SharedInformer and related controller.
465: // Because returning information back is always asynchronous, the legacy callers shouldn't
466: // notice any change in behavior.
467: type dummyController struct {
468: 	informer *sharedIndexInformer
469: }

505: 		handler(r, err)

509: func (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext(handler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext) error {
510: 	s.startedLock.Lock()
511: 	defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
512: 
513: 	if s.started {
514: 		return fmt.Errorf("informer has already started")
515: 	}
516: 
517: 	s.watchErrorHandler = handler
518: 	return nil
519: }

521: func (s *sharedIndexInformer) SetTransform(handler TransformFunc) error {
522: 	s.startedLock.Lock()
523: 	defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
524: 
525: 	if s.started {
526: 		return fmt.Errorf("informer has already started")
527: 	}
528: 
529: 	s.transform = handler
530: 	return nil
531: }

533: func (s *sharedIndexInformer) Run(stopCh <-chan struct{}) {

537: func (s *sharedIndexInformer) RunWithContext(ctx context.Context) {
538: 	defer utilruntime.HandleCrashWithContext(ctx)
539: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
540: 
541: 	if s.HasStarted() {
542: 		logger.Info("Warning: the sharedIndexInformer has started, run more than once is not allowed")
543: 		return
544: 	}
545: 
546: 	func() {
547: 		s.startedLock.Lock()
548: 		defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
549: 
550: 		fifo := newQueueFIFO(s.indexer, s.transform)
551: 
552: 		cfg := &Config{
553: 			Queue:             fifo,
554: 			ListerWatcher:     s.listerWatcher,
555: 			ObjectType:        s.objectType,
556: 			ObjectDescription: s.objectDescription,
557: 			FullResyncPeriod:  s.resyncCheckPeriod,
558: 			ShouldResync:      s.processor.shouldResync,
559: 
560: 			Process:                      s.HandleDeltas,
561: 			ProcessBatch:                 s.HandleBatchDeltas,
562: 			WatchErrorHandlerWithContext: s.watchErrorHandler,
563: 		}
564: 
565: 		s.controller = New(cfg)
566: 		s.controller.(*controller).clock = s.clock
567: 		s.started = true
568: 	}()
569: 
570: 	// Separate stop context because Processor should be stopped strictly after controller.
571: 	// Cancelation in the parent context is ignored and all values are passed on,
572: 	// including - but not limited to - a logger.
573: 	processorStopCtx, stopProcessor := context.WithCancelCause(context.WithoutCancel(ctx))
574: 	var wg wait.Group
575: 	defer wg.Wait()                                         // Wait for Processor to stop
576: 	defer stopProcessor(errors.New("informer is stopping")) // Tell Processor to stop
577: 	// TODO: extend the MutationDetector interface so that it optionally
578: 	// has a RunWithContext method that we can use here.
579: 	wg.StartWithChannel(processorStopCtx.Done(), s.cacheMutationDetector.Run)
580: 	wg.StartWithContext(processorStopCtx, s.processor.run)
581: 
582: 	defer func() {
583: 		s.startedLock.Lock()
584: 		defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
585: 		s.stopped = true // Don't want any new listeners
586: 	}()
587: 	s.controller.RunWithContext(ctx)
588: }
589:

591: 	s.startedLock.Lock()
592: 	defer s.startedLock.Unlock()
593: 	return s.started
594: }

30: // Lister is any object that knows how to perform an initial list.
31: //
32: // Ideally, all implementations of Lister should also implement ListerWithContext.
33: type Lister interface {
34: 	// List should return a list type object; the Items field will be extracted, and the
35: 	// ResourceVersion field will be used to start the watch in the right place.
36: 	//
37: 	// Deprecated: use ListerWithContext.ListWithContext instead.
38: 	List(options metav1.ListOptions) (runtime.Object, error)
39: }
40: 
41: // ListerWithContext is any object that knows how to perform an initial list.
42: type ListerWithContext interface {
43: 	// ListWithContext should return a list type object; the Items field will be extracted, and the
44: 	// ResourceVersion field will be used to start the watch in the right place.
45: 	ListWithContext(ctx context.Context, options metav1.ListOptions) (runtime.Object, error)
46: }
47: 
48: func ToListerWithContext(l Lister) ListerWithContext {
49: 	if l, ok := l.(ListerWithContext); ok {
50: 		return l
51: 	}
52: 	return listerWrapper{
53: 		parent: l,
54: 	}
55: }

59: }
60:

62: 	return l.parent.List(options)
63: }

65: // Watcher is any object that knows how to start a watch on a resource.
66: //
67: // Ideally, all implementations of Watcher should also implement WatcherWithContext.
68: type Watcher interface {
69: 	// Watch should begin a watch at the specified version.
70: 	//
71: 	// If Watch returns an error, it should handle its own cleanup, including
72: 	// but not limited to calling Stop() on the watch, if one was constructed.
73: 	// This allows the caller to ignore the watch, if the error is non-nil.
74: 	//
75: 	// Deprecated: use WatcherWithContext.WatchWithContext instead.
76: 	Watch(options metav1.ListOptions) (watch.Interface, error)
77: }
78: 
79: // WatcherWithContext is any object that knows how to start a watch on a resource.
80: type WatcherWithContext interface {
81: 	// WatchWithContext should begin a watch at the specified version.
82: 	//
83: 	// If Watch returns an error, it should handle its own cleanup, including
84: 	// but not limited to calling Stop() on the watch, if one was constructed.
85: 	// This allows the caller to ignore the watch, if the error is non-nil.
86: 	WatchWithContext(ctx context.Context, options metav1.ListOptions) (watch.Interface, error)
87: }

89: func ToWatcherWithContext(w Watcher) WatcherWithContext {
90: 	if w, ok := w.(WatcherWithContext); ok {
91: 		return w
92: 	}
93: 	return watcherWrapper{
94: 		parent: w,
95: 	}
96: }

99: 	parent Watcher
100: }

28: // ThreadSafeStore is an interface that allows concurrent indexed
29: // access to a storage backend.  It is like Indexer but does not
30: // (necessarily) know how to extract the Store key from a given
31: // object.
32: //
33: // TL;DR caveats: you must not modify anything returned by Get or List as it will break
34: // the indexing feature in addition to not being thread safe.
35: //
36: // The guarantees of thread safety provided by List/Get are only valid if the caller
37: // treats returned items as read-only. For example, a pointer inserted in the store
38: // through `Add` will be returned as is by `Get`. Multiple clients might invoke `Get`
39: // on the same key and modify the pointer in a non-thread-safe way. Also note that
40: // modifying objects stored by the indexers (if any) will *not* automatically lead
41: // to a re-index. So it's not a good idea to directly modify the objects returned by
42: // Get/List, in general.
43: type ThreadSafeStore interface {
44: 	Add(key string, obj interface{})
45: 	Update(key string, obj interface{})
46: 	Delete(key string)
47: 	Get(key string) (item interface{}, exists bool)
48: 	List() []interface{}
49: 	ListKeys() []string
50: 	Replace(map[string]interface{}, string)
51: 	Index(indexName string, obj interface{}) ([]interface{}, error)
52: 	IndexKeys(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]string, error)
53: 	ListIndexFuncValues(name string) []string
54: 	ByIndex(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]interface{}, error)
55: 	GetIndexers() Indexers
56: 
57: 	// AddIndexers adds more indexers to this store. This supports adding indexes after the store already has items.
58: 	AddIndexers(newIndexers Indexers) error
59: 	// Resync is a no-op and is deprecated
60: 	Resync() error
61: }
62: 
63: // ThreadSafeStoreWithTransaction is a store that can batch execute multiple transactions.
64: type ThreadSafeStoreWithTransaction interface {
65: 	ThreadSafeStore
66: 	// Transaction allows performing multiple writes in one call.
67: 	Transaction(fns ...ThreadSafeStoreTransaction)
68: }
69: 
70: // ThreadSafeStoreTransaction embeds a Transaction and includes the specific Key identifying the affected object.
71: type ThreadSafeStoreTransaction struct {
72: 	Transaction
73: 	Key string
74: }
75: 
76: // storeIndex implements the indexing functionality for Store interface
77: type storeIndex struct {
78: 	// indexers maps a name to an IndexFunc
79: 	indexers Indexers
80: 	// indices maps a name to an Index
81: 	indices Indices
82: }
83: 
84: func (i *storeIndex) reset() {
85: 	i.indices = Indices{}
86: }
87: 
88: func (i *storeIndex) getKeysFromIndex(indexName string, obj interface{}) (sets.Set[string], error) {
89: 	indexFunc := i.indexers[indexName]
90: 	if indexFunc == nil {
91: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Index with name %s does not exist", indexName)
92: 	}
93: 
94: 	indexedValues, err := indexFunc(obj)
95: 	if err != nil {
96: 		return nil, err
97: 	}
98: 	index := i.indices[indexName]
99: 
100: 	var storeKeySet sets.Set[string]

30: // IsRetryableErrorFunc allows the client to provide its own function
31: // that determines whether the specified err from the server is retryable.
32: //
33: // request: the original request sent to the server
34: // err: the server sent this error to us
35: //
36: // The function returns true if the error is retryable and the request
37: // can be retried, otherwise it returns false.
38: // We have four mode of communications - 'Stream', 'Watch', 'Do' and 'DoRaw', this
39: // function allows us to customize the retryability aspect of each.
40: type IsRetryableErrorFunc func(request *http.Request, err error) bool
41: 
42: func (r IsRetryableErrorFunc) IsErrorRetryable(request *http.Request, err error) bool {
43: 	return r(request, err)
44: }
45: 
46: var neverRetryError = IsRetryableErrorFunc(func(_ *http.Request, _ error) bool {
47: 	return false
48: })
49: 
50: // WithRetry allows the client to retry a request up to a certain number of times
51: // Note that WithRetry is not safe for concurrent use by multiple
52: // goroutines without additional locking or coordination.
53: type WithRetry interface {
54: 	// IsNextRetry advances the retry counter appropriately
55: 	// and returns true if the request should be retried,
56: 	// otherwise it returns false, if:
57: 	//  - we have already reached the maximum retry threshold.
58: 	//  - the error does not fall into the retryable category.
59: 	//  - the server has not sent us a 429, or 5xx status code and the
60: 	//    'Retry-After' response header is not set with a value.
61: 	//  - we need to seek to the beginning of the request body before we
62: 	//    initiate the next retry, the function should log an error and
63: 	//    return false if it fails to do so.
64: 	//
65: 	// restReq: the associated rest.Request
66: 	// httpReq: the HTTP Request sent to the server
67: 	// resp: the response sent from the server, it is set if err is nil
68: 	// err: the server sent this error to us, if err is set then resp is nil.
69: 	// f: a IsRetryableErrorFunc function provided by the client that determines
70: 	//    if the err sent by the server is retryable.
71: 	IsNextRetry(ctx context.Context, restReq *Request, httpReq *http.Request, resp *http.Response, err error, f IsRetryableErrorFunc) bool
72: 
73: 	// Before should be invoked prior to each attempt, including
74: 	// the first one. If an error is returned, the request should
75: 	// be aborted immediately.
76: 	//
77: 	// Before may also be additionally responsible for preparing
78: 	// the request for the next retry, namely in terms of resetting
79: 	// the request body in case it has been read.
80: 	Before(ctx context.Context, r *Request) error
81: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kubernetes--8c9c67c0@8c9c67c000104450cfc5a5f48053a9a84b73cf93/-/blob/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/with_retry.go%3FL1-80"}

34: // MutationCache is able to take the result of update operations and stores them in an LRU
35: // that can be used to provide a more current view of a requested object.  It requires interpreting
36: // resourceVersions for comparisons.
37: // Implementations must be thread-safe.
38: // TODO find a way to layer this into an informer/lister
39: type MutationCache interface {
40: 	GetByKey(key string) (interface{}, bool, error)
41: 	ByIndex(indexName, indexKey string) ([]interface{}, error)
42: 	Mutation(interface{})
43: }
44: 
45: // ResourceVersionComparator is able to compare object versions.
46: type ResourceVersionComparator interface {
47: 	CompareResourceVersion(lhs, rhs runtime.Object) int
48: }
49: 
50: // NewIntegerResourceVersionMutationCache returns a MutationCache that understands how to
51: // deal with objects that have a resource version that:
52: //
53: //   - is an integer
54: //   - increases when updated
55: //   - is comparable across the same resource in a namespace
56: //
57: // Most backends will have these semantics. Indexer may be nil. ttl controls how long an item
58: // remains in the mutation cache before it is removed.
59: //
60: // If includeAdds is true, objects in the mutation cache will be returned even if they don't exist

23: // Every feature gate should have an entry here following this template:
24: //
25: // // owner: @username
26: // // alpha: v1.4
27: // MyFeature featuregate.Feature = "MyFeature"
28: //
29: // Feature gates should be listed in alphabetical, case-sensitive
30: // (upper before any lower case character) order. This reduces the risk
31: // of code conflicts because changes are more likely to be scattered
32: // across the file.
33: const (
34: 	// owner: @michaelasp
35: 	// beta: v1.36
36: 	//
37: 	// Allow the client to process events atomically rather than a stream of
38: 	// events for items popped off the FIFO.
39: 	AtomicFIFO Feature = "AtomicFIFO"
40: 
41: 	// owner: @benluddy
42: 	// kep: https://kep.k8s.io/4222
43: 	// alpha: 1.32
44: 	//
45: 	// If disabled, clients configured to accept "application/cbor" will instead accept
46: 	// "application/json" with the same relative preference, and clients configured to write
47: 	// "application/cbor" or "application/apply-patch+cbor" will instead write
48: 	// "application/json" or "application/apply-patch+yaml", respectively.
49: 	ClientsAllowCBOR Feature = "ClientsAllowCBOR"
50: 
51: 	// owner: @benluddy
52: 	// kep: https://kep.k8s.io/4222
53: 	// alpha: 1.32
54: 	//
55: 	// If enabled, and only if ClientsAllowCBOR is also enabled, the default request content
56: 	// type (if not explicitly configured) and the dynamic client's request content type both
57: 	// become "application/cbor" instead of "application/json". The default content type for
58: 	// apply patch requests becomes "application/apply-patch+cbor" instead of
59: 	// "application/apply-patch+yaml".
